Editorial

The Jonjo Shelvey Conundrum

Arsenal · 2026-07-22

ArsenalGabriel

Years ago, my friend, Henry, told me about one of his Career Mode saves. Managing Swansea City, he managed to essentially break Jonjo Shelvey, turning him into a 91 rated CM with 4 Premier League Titles, 2 Champions League Titles, and a Ballon D’Or under his belt.

One summer, after half of a decade of service, Shelvey requested a transfer. Being magnanimous, Henry was willing to allow him a dream move. Eventually, Barcelona came knocking, in true Catalan style, with £24m. “No,” Henry says. "That’s insane. He’s 27. He just won the Ballon D’Or." Counter. £75m. Barca are having none of it.

£28m, they reply. This doesn’t make any sense. “What kind of Cesc Fabregas bullshit is going on,” he laughs to me. £68m, he counters-back.

£35m. Final offer. Henry stopped dead in his tracks.

For a player of such stature, £35m was comically low. It made no sense. But in what world did it makes sense for Jonjo Shevley to have earned a move to Barcelona? Perhaps Anthony Gordon has shown us anything is possible.

Henry accepted the offer. Barcelona, even in a fantasy, knew there was something afoot about the entire Shelvey evolution, and the money didn’t matter to Henry, who’d already banked an essentially limitless transfer budget. So off to Catalonia Jonjo went, where he reverted back to the 78 he always was within three seasons.

Mikel Arteta has broken Gabriel. He turned a 76 (with 82 potential) into a legit 86 with intangibles that make him greater than the sum of his statistics. He is a threat, though that has far more to do with the delivery, from set-pieces and is a fearless defender of the box in a low/mid-block.

He also benefits greatly from playing next to William Saliba, who constantly covers for him technically. And that’s a problem in what is still, despite misconceptions, a possession-heavy side. The Brazilian was not helped with Calafiori’s absence, as his partnership on the left side with Hincapie was often brutal on the ball, and a main point of defeat in Arsenal’s inability to get a foot-hold against PSG.

The Brazilian, ever robust, has shown slight signs of physical decline over the past seasons, and it wouldn't be surprising if his athletic peak is behind him. For a player like Gabriel, a CB that defends man-to-man better than he defends space, that could be costly and his lack of advanced ability on the ball also means he may not age as gracefully. Still, at every turning point he has risen to the occasion and broken through the glass ceiling that others may have imposed. It's not impossible he does this again.

Regardless, now is, at least theoretically, the perfect time to sell in the even of a big money offer, say £75m. Gabriel is far more replaceable internally—Calafiori, Hincapie—than perhaps realized, and there are a bevy of LCBs that could be brought in at a reasonable price—Schlotterbeck, Bastoni, O.Diomande, Lukeba.

In reality, the potential transfer is more complicated. William Saliba's anticipated long term absence means Arsenal's focus needs to be on incoming players, and losing Gabriel's experience and guaranteed floor of performance would be risky. He is also an emotional pillar of the team, and while there are leaders abound, upsetting the emotional dynamic of a squad recently united by hard-won victory may not be wise.

In different circumstances, this would be the summer that an elite-level franchise would sell-high on an overvalued asset. Bill Belichick style. In Arsenal's circumstance, the move can understandably delayed a season, but there could be a legitimate loss in profit upon re-visitation.

TransfersGrades, rumors, recommendations, and proposed depth charts

Transfer Grade by team

Transfer Grade

Arsenal — Transfer Grades

2026-07-23 · Arsenal

Published
Arsenal

IN:

Ilan Meslier, free
- In me, you will find no great fan of Kepa Arrizabalaga. In fact, sometimes I believe he was built in a laboratory to frustrate me. I don’t think he’s awful. I’ve seen awful. Manuel Almunia flapping about. David Ospina standing behind the goal-line on corner kicks. But Kepa is a tough-watch. He is both loud and small, agile but slow, confident yet anxious. He was also poor in high-leverage moments, which are essentially all that exist to a back-up keeper.
Andrea Berta has somehow found a keeper than inspires more anxiety and has a bevy of high-profile errors to his numerous stints as Leeds #1. Like Alphonse Areola at West Ham, he was caught in a vicious cycle of being the main man and being almost entirely ostracized. I don't trust him, and I don't buy him as either the stable veteran presence or the successor to Raya's shirt.

Grade: F

Christos Tzolis, £35m
- The Greek winger's p/90 comparables are eye-catching. 100% chances created, 96% shot attempts, 91% goals. The Juliper Pro League is not Europe's premier competition, but it shows Tzolis as a well-rounded player. On film, his athleticism and intention— both driving with the ball and running off it, pops off the screen. As a depth option, he provides spark and incision, though his skillset more clearly overlaps with Gabriel Martinelli than it does the recently departed Leandro Trossard.
In today's market, this deal could end up looking like a steal. If Tzolis doesn't adjust to the demands of elite-level football, it's not a fee that precludes any sort of future outlay

Grade: B+

Elijah Upson, free
- Signing high-level U21 for free is nothing to roll your eyes at. Whether or not Upson can develop into a Premier League level defender, I have my doubts. Taking him from Spurs is nice, but they've done a bit of damage to us as well, snatching away Kyran Thompson.

Grade: B

OUT:

Leandro Trossard, £17m (Besiktas)
- Arsenal have decided to move on from the Belgian, who has been the preferred LW option for at least the past two seasons. The fee is not significant. Questions of whether Christos Tzolis, who cost £35m, will perform at a higher level than Trossard are valid. Given the former Arsenal man's levels of performance, particularly in high-leverage moments, I'm surprised at the willingness to sell so early in the window for such an uninspiring fee.
Grade: C-

10

ST
Position Guide What each role actually means
GK
Goalkeeper
Cat
Reflex oriented. Slick on the ball. Examples: David Raya, Keylor Navas, Caoimhin Kelleher
Monster
Physically imposing monsters that command the box and envelope the goal. Examples: Thibaut Courtois, Emi Martinez, Gianluigi Donnarumma
CB
Centre Back
Libero
"Sweeper," more accurately described as a Defender of Space. Asked to read/cover huge parts of the field. Requires elite IQ, athleticism, and amongst elite teams, technical quality. The rarest of CB types. Examples: Virgil van Dijk, William Saliba
Zaguero
The defender of the man. The much more common, pugnacious, ball-winning defender. Aggressive, steps into midfield to press. Imposing physically and mentally. Examples: Gabriel, Ibrahima Konate
FB
Full Back
Wide Defender
CB/FB hybrid. Can comfortably play at one/both FB positions, as well as CB. Athleticism and technique required. With a shift towards pragmatism and total football, this is a very valuable player type when building a squad. Examples: Nathan Ake, Ben White, Jurrien Timber
Wing Back
WM/FB hybrid. Sets the width in the attacking third. Requires elite fitness, top level crossing, and intelligent back post runs. Examples: Federico Dimarco, Denzel Dumfries
Inverted Full Back
CM/FB hybrid. An outside back that, in possession, tucks into midfield to create overloads. Requires elite technique, and usually training/experience in central midfield. Examples: Phillip Lahm, Oleskandr Zinchenko, Rico Lewis
CM
Central Midfielder
Regista
The deep-lying playmaker, the 'architect' of the attack. Must be elegant on the half turn and press-resistant. Requires a complete range of passing. The fitness it requires makes leaner body-types more common. Examples: Sergio Busquets, Frenkie De Jong, Ayyoub Bouaddi
Anchor
A form of '6', and when done correctly sets the physical standard of the team. Also requires elite ability at breaking up play. Advanced versions are also able to connect play. Examples: Rodri (advanced), Casemiro (regular)
Box-to-Box
Also called Carrilero. The 'shuttler' of the midfield. A ball-winning midfielder positioned further up the pitch. Ideally can carry the ball. Athletic ability crucial. Examples: Gavi, Park Ji-Sung, Arturo Vidal
Complete CM
A player that successfully embodies an advanced version of Regista/Anchor/Box-to-Box AND Eganche/Mezzala/Trequartista. Examples: Luka Modric, Warren Zaire-Emery, Cesc Fabregas
AM/10
Attacking Midfielder / No. 10
Eganche
"The hook," the stationary, central pivot. Sets the attacking tempo. The most attacking player within a more conservative midfield composition. Examples: Yaya Toure, Juan Roman Riquelme, Vitinha
Mezzala
An attacking midfielder that thrives in the half-space between the flank and the center. Requires elite ball-striking in shots, crosses, and through balls, which means the ability to use all surfaces and angles of the foot/ball. Examples: Kevin De Bruyne, Samir Nasri, Bernardo Silva
Trequartista
Italian for "3/4" -- signifying the space between midfield and the attacking third. The Germans, ever literal call it a "Raumdeuter," which means space interpreter. It requires great intuition and understanding of the game. Can be described as the "Classic #10," as well. Will wander wide. Examples: Francesco Totti, Thomas Muller, Dani Olmo
W
Winger
Winger
Holds the width of the attack. There are 4 types: - Runner, threat in behind - Breaker, 1v1 - Creator, playmaking - Magician, all 3-in-1 Examples: - Anthony Gordon (runner) - Angel di Maria (breaker) - Leandro Trossard (creator) - Kvicha Kvaratskhelia (magician) - Nico Williams (runner-breaker) - Michael Olise (creator-breaker) - Iliman Ndiaye (runner-creator)
Wide Forward
A winger with a level of finishing that demands they play further up the pitch, even without the ball. Often this requires an elite 'Breaker' trait, as well. Examples: Mohamed Salah, Heung-Min Son, Raphinha
F
Forward
Second Striker
While the implication is slightly different, functionally the same as "False 9." The most advanced central player in a team. Often creates overloads in midfield. Requires instinct and power, as well as great technical ability. Examples: Wayne Rooney, Ousmane Dembele, Lionel Messi
#9
A Centre Forward that can hold the ball up as well as run in behind. That is stated simply, but it's quite rare. Examples: Luis Suarez, Thierry Henry, Samuel Eto'o
Target Man
Wants the ball to feet. Usually requires size & technical prowess. Threat in the air. Examples: Olivier Giroud, Edin Dzeko, Romelu Lukaku
Striker
"Poacher," is an over-simplification. A player that thrives off the last shoulder. The final action. The "right place, right time," guy/gal. Examples: Erling Haaland (advanced), Viktor Gyokeres (regular)
Streets Won't Forget Cult heroes. Tournament icons. Your XI.

EditorialsLonger pieces, arguments, obsessions, and football writing.

Browse editorials

Arsenal

The Jonjo Shelvey Conundrum

2026-07-22

Published
ArsenalArsenalGabriel

Years ago, my friend, Henry, told me about one of his Career Mode saves. Managing Swansea City, he managed to essentially break Jonjo Shelvey, turning him into a 91 rated CM with 4 Premier League Titles, 2 Champions League Titles, and a Ballon D’Or under his belt.

One summer, after half of a decade of service, Shelvey requested a transfer. Being magnanimous, Henry was willing to allow him a dream move. Eventually, Barcelona came knocking, in true Catalan style, with £24m. “No,” Henry says. "That’s insane. He’s 27. He just won the Ballon D’Or." Counter. £75m. Barca are having none of it.

£28m, they reply. This doesn’t make any sense. “What kind of Cesc Fabregas bullshit is going on,” he laughs to me. £68m, he counters-back.

£35m. Final offer. Henry stopped dead in his tracks.

For a player of such stature, £35m was comically low. It made no sense. But in what world did it makes sense for Jonjo Shevley to have earned a move to Barcelona? Perhaps Anthony Gordon has shown us anything is possible.

Henry accepted the offer. Barcelona, even in a fantasy, knew there was something afoot about the entire Shelvey evolution, and the money didn’t matter to Henry, who’d already banked an essentially limitless transfer budget. So off to Catalonia Jonjo went, where he reverted back to the 78 he always was within three seasons.

Mikel Arteta has broken Gabriel. He turned a 76 (with 82 potential) into a legit 86 with intangibles that make him greater than the sum of his statistics. He is a threat, though that has far more to do with the delivery, from set-pieces and is a fearless defender of the box in a low/mid-block.

He also benefits greatly from playing next to William Saliba, who constantly covers for him technically. And that’s a problem in what is still, despite misconceptions, a possession-heavy side. The Brazilian was not helped with Calafiori’s absence, as his partnership on the left side with Hincapie was often brutal on the ball, and a main point of defeat in Arsenal’s inability to get a foot-hold against PSG.

The Brazilian, ever robust, has shown slight signs of physical decline over the past seasons, and it wouldn't be surprising if his athletic peak is behind him. For a player like Gabriel, a CB that defends man-to-man better than he defends space, that could be costly and his lack of advanced ability on the ball also means he may not age as gracefully. Still, at every turning point he has risen to the occasion and broken through the glass ceiling that others may have imposed. It's not impossible he does this again.

Regardless, now is, at least theoretically, the perfect time to sell in the even of a big money offer, say £75m. Gabriel is far more replaceable internally—Calafiori, Hincapie—than perhaps realized, and there are a bevy of LCBs that could be brought in at a reasonable price—Schlotterbeck, Bastoni, O.Diomande, Lukeba.

In reality, the potential transfer is more complicated. William Saliba's anticipated long term absence means Arsenal's focus needs to be on incoming players, and losing Gabriel's experience and guaranteed floor of performance would be risky. He is also an emotional pillar of the team, and while there are leaders abound, upsetting the emotional dynamic of a squad recently united by hard-won victory may not be wise.

In different circumstances, this would be the summer that an elite-level franchise would sell-high on an overvalued asset. Bill Belichick style. In Arsenal's circumstance, the move can understandably delayed a season, but there could be a legitimate loss in profit upon re-visitation.

Matchday Diary Games watched · thoughts written

Browse diaries

World Cup Final · Spain d. Argentina (1-0 a.e.t)

20 Years Later

2026-07-19

Published
World Cup Final

My first memories of sports came in the Summer of 2006. The Heat and Mavericks were playing in the NBA Finals. I remember feeling conflicted. I wanted Miami to win after the Mavs went up 2-0, but something about the refereeing in the ensuing games sat poorly with me.

In those days, my summers were filled with Basketball and Baseball— Soccer was an afterthought. The day of the Final, my Father invited over some Dads from my 1st grade class to come watch. He always bought the absolute best television available. Back then, a nice TV was less common.

My Dad began supporting Arsenal in the 90s. At the time, only a few teams were consistently shown on TV in the US... Man United, Liverpool, Arsenal, and he took to Dennis Bergkamp and Ian Wright. He wasn't much of a player, but when he taught Physics at Sewickley Academy he was also the Assistant Coach, underneath my German Godfather Uwe, of the Varsity Soccer Team. They won a State Championship and it bonded him to the sport. RIP Lenny Muckle.

In his red Arsenal cap, he sat me down, explaining that on France was Arsenal's, our favorite team, best player, Thierry Henry. He was someone important, someone elegant, someone who scored great goals. There was also Zidane, my father explained. An enigma with an amazing first touch. And what a touch that penalty was, careening off the crossbar, landing just over the goaline before spinning out the complete opposite direction. To my eyes, though I can only describe it as such in hindsight, it seemed metaphysical, other-wordly, what he had made the ball do. My father jumped out of his seat. He was in awe of what he saw and so was I. This was sprezzatura.

Much of the rest of the game was a blur of blue and white bodies and I couldn't understand more than that it really mattered. Then out of nothing, confusion, a red-card. What happened? What's a red card? The replay begins. Zidane planting his forehead into Materazzi's chest. Why? What did he say? Why didn't that hurt his head? The pan to Zizou walking off the pitch, in his golden boots, past the Golden Trophy. It was like a movie. Seconds later, it seemed, Grosso buried the final penalty in the right-side netting. Henry's disappointed face flashed across the screen. What I wanted to happen did not happen. Italy had won and it didn't feel good. 20 years later, and soccer's grip on my feelings has only tightened.

Sunday was the Spain v. Argentina Final. It was also my Girlfriend's Birthday. A bit of a conundrum. And it was off to a shaky start. Her idea was to have us watch at some huge Spanish gathering point with her two Spaniard x Miami friends. She showed me the view of the single screen they had. Maybe 200 feet away, hundreds of people around them. And this was two and a half hours before the match.

Besides not being able to actually see the game, the thought of being around Spanish people supporting Spain didn't sit right with me. Rooting against Messi felt wrong. Rooting for Argentina also didn't feel right. And Cesca, a Brazilian still feeling the aftereffects of another doomed Hexa Campaign, did not want Argentina to win. This meant I needed to find a way to explain either result to myself. So I came up with the following:

I'd rather Argentina win, because that's Messi going back-to-back. That's God's son. If Spain win, let it be Yamal that makes 'the play'. Because then it's God's son's son. And that also makes sense. It almost was.

After a dominant 90+ minutes, filled with shots fired directly at Emi Martinez, Spain won a free kick from about 25 yards out in the relative center of the pitch. We were watching from the tables directly outside Beco, a Brazilian restaurant in Williamsburg. The projector was grainy, and heads populated the lower reaches of the screen, but at the right angle I could more or less get a clear picture of the game.

Lamine Yamal and Pedro Porro stood over it. I turned towards Kevin, the 6'2" Ecuadorian and the woman he sat across from, who I at first assumed was his date before seeing Kev greet three different women, as well as Cesca and said, "Don't let that imp Porro take this. It has to be Lamine. It has to be him."

Yamal hit the ball well, avoiding the wall to Martinez's strong side while getting good height, whip and speed. Dibu read it and made what was a very good save to his left hand side. It should have been the goal to win the match. It was a worthy player, and a worthy strike of the ball. But Martinez stood tall.

Instead, it was "Make Spain Great Again's," Ferran Torres in the first half of Extra Time. Argentina had nothing to offer the match, even before Enzo Fernandez's red card. They didn't deserve to win. Still, the final whistle left me with a hollow feeling, as did the "Brasil," chants that followed.

I enjoyed the rest of the night with Cesca and her Spanish friends, who we met up with after, and didn't think too much about the game. I watched videos of Keyne, Yamal's 3-year old brother, and that made me feel a bit better. The 19 year old is an incredibly likable figure, and displays great grace and skill in the biggest moments. He also stood on business, again, reminding the French and the public before their Semi-Final, when asked, that Spain should be looked at as favorites.

I was still sad for Messi. Not because Argentina had lost, but because he never got the opportunity to make a difference. I remember feeling the same way about France in 2006 after my Dad told me that if Zidane was on the field to take a penalty, France may have won. I remember feeling that way just two months ago watching Arsenal lose to PSG on penalties. It's my most familiar feeling watching football.
